def: The central question that a study or investigation sets out to answer.
A statement put forward as an idea to be argued and defended.
A small group selected to represent a larger whole in a study.
The careful examination of data to draw conclusions.
A piece of knowledge gained through study or reflection.
A note stating where information or a quotation comes from.
To work out or trace something logically from a source or principle.
Able to be understood or followed step by step.
Firmly based on solid knowledge or evidence.
The quality of being honest and holding firm moral principles.
The act of presenting someone else's work or ideas as one's own.
A twisting of facts that gives a false or one-sided picture.
A clash between one's duty and one's personal interests.
Openness that allows others to see how decisions are made.
To influence or alter something dishonestly for one's own ends.
To make a false copy or alter something in order to deceive.
Open to doubt; likely to be unreliable or improper.
The work of explaining research to a wider public.
A person without expert knowledge of a particular subject.
A doubting attitude that questions claims before accepting them.
The quality of being easy to understand.
Financial or practical support given to help something develop.
To pass on knowledge or a message; also to mediate.
Widely accepted as valid or of good standing.
Expressed so that everyone can understand it.
The whole set of words a person or language has.
The rules that govern how words combine in a language.
The way in which a word or language is spoken.
A local form of a language with its own words and sounds.
The first language a person learns as a child.
A person's overall ability to use a language.
To gain or come to possess something through effort.
Able to speak or write a language easily and smoothly.
An indirect sign that suggests something without stating it.
The way the voice sounds, showing feeling or attitude.
Behaviour that shows good manners and respect.
A failure to understand something correctly.
An indirect reference to something without naming it directly.
To suggest something indirectly rather than stating it.
To express something using different words.
Easily understood in more than one way; open to confusion.
Suggested or understood without being directly stated.
A language given legal status for use in government.
The effort to maintain and protect the quality of a language.
An English word or phrase borrowed into another language.
The existence of many different languages in a place.
The perceived worsening or decay of a language over time.
To keep something safe and unchanged over time.
To cease to exist completely, as a species or language.
Setting out how things should be, not just how they are.
A standard or rule that is regarded as usual or expected.
A firmly held belief or opinion.
The fulfilment of one's own talents and potential.
An awareness of and respect for inherited customs.
A time of great and sudden change.
To gradually take on a different form or character.
No longer suitable because newer things have replaced it.
Fairness between the young and the old, now and in the future.
The large generation born in the years after the Second World War.
The mood and ideas that define a particular period.
The manner in which a person or group lives.
A deep gap or difference between people or groups.
To grow apart from someone or something once close.
Having a strong, lasting influence on how something develops.
A group of citizens organising to influence a public issue.
Money or goods given freely to help a cause.
A public expression of strong objection.
The right to take part in decisions that affect one.
To take an active part in something one cares about.
To work actively in support of a person or cause.
Working for the public good rather than for profit.
Showing strong commitment and active involvement.
The person who receives something, such as a letter or payment.
The line in a letter or email that states its topic.
The words used to address someone at the start of a message.
A document sent with a letter; also an investment or a facility.
A written record of what was said and decided at a meeting.
To put an idea into carefully chosen words.
A matter that someone wishes to raise or have dealt with.
A reason or set of reasons given to support a view.
A reason given against a proposal or idea.
Something granted or given up to reach agreement.
The particular way a person views an issue.
The power to make others believe or agree.
To make someone believe something or agree to do it.
To prove that a statement or argument is wrong.
Well reasoned and able to stand up to scrutiny.
A person who holds a leading position in an organisation.
The reason or drive that makes someone act.
The recognition of someone's or something's worth.
A serious disagreement or struggle between sides.
To give someone a reason or drive to act.
Showing recognition and respect for others.
Government measures aimed at tackling climate change.
The maximum permitted level of something, such as a pollutant.
A tax charged on the carbon dioxide that fuels release.
A formal arrangement agreed between parties or countries.
An official condition that must be met; also a print run.
To put a plan or decision into practice.
To keep to a rule, promise, or deadline.
The inner sense of what is morally right and wrong.
A reason given to show that something is right or reasonable.
A difficult choice between two equally undesirable options.
Something one is morally or legally bound to do.
The quality of being believable and trustworthy.
To consider different factors carefully before deciding.
To show or argue that something is right or reasonable.
Relating to right and wrong behaviour.
A single person considered separately from the group.
The duty to answer for one's own actions and choices.
A deep change affecting a whole system rather than its parts.
The change or result produced by a cause.
A lasting change in the basic make-up of an economy or society.
To cause something to happen.
To help bring about a result.
Producing no effect or result.
The worldwide buying and selling of goods and services.
The splitting of work into separate tasks done by different people.
A benefit a place offers for business over others.
The state of relying on someone or something; also addiction.
The moving of work to an outside company, often abroad.
To link things closely together so they affect each other.
Extending across the border between countries.
The reaching of understanding or agreement between people.
A comprehensive view of life and the world.
The ability to adjust well to new conditions.
A fine instinct for handling people or situations delicately.
A lack of impartiality; also awkward self-awareness.
To imagine oneself in another person's situation.
Willing to consider new ideas and experiences.
